title: Transport signatures of valley polarization in graphene multilayers: In-plane linear magnetoconductivity vs anomalous Hall effect
abstract: In two-dimensional materials where interacting Fermi pockets occur in valleys related by time-reversal symmetry, a spontaneous valley imbalance results in a novel state known as an orbital magnet. Due to the breaking of time-reversal symmetry, this state can be probed in transport experiments by the violation of Onsager relations, most often done through the anomalous Hall effect (AHE). Here we propose that odd-in-field, in-plane linear magnetoconductivity (LMC) is an alternative probe of valley polarization which can occur even when the AHE vanishes. In multilayer structures, the effect originates from in-plane orbital moments and Berry curvatures enabled by interlayer tunneling and dominates over the spin response. After a classification of many recently studied multilayers, we focus on two valley polarized examples: twisted bilayer graphene, where LMC is finite but the AHE vanishes unless additional symmetry breaking from the substrate is present, and rhombohedral graphene multilayers, where LMC and AHE both track valley polarization because they have the same symmetry. Using self-consistent Hartree-Fock and semiclassical transport calculations, we present detailed predictions of LMR for these two examples and analyze the implications for recent experiments.
summary: This theoretical work establishes that in 2D graphene multilayers, the in-plane linear magnetoconductivity (LMC) serves as a superior and complementary diagnostic tool to the Anomalous Hall Effect (AHE) for detecting spontaneous valley polarization. By performing detailed symmetry and transport calculations, the authors show that LMC remains finite even when AHE vanishes, providing a pathway to characterize exotic correlated electronic states.
main_problem: The paper investigates how to reliably probe spontaneous valley polarization (VP) in 2D graphene multilayers, especially when the standard diagnostic, the Anomalous Hall Effect (AHE), might vanish due to symmetry.
main_result: In-plane linear magnetoconductivity (LMC) is proposed as a unique and faithful alternative probe for VP, as it remains finite even when AHE is suppressed by symmetry or disorder.
method: The study employs symmetry analysis using point group theory and semiclassical transport calculations (Boltzmann formalism) to derive and compare the theoretical expressions for LMC and AHE.
model_or_system: The focus is on graphene multilayers, including Twisted Bilayer Graphene (TBG) and rhombohedral graphene (RG), modeled using continuum Hamiltonians like the Koshino-McCann model, incorporating electron-electron interactions.
key_observables: In-plane linear magnetoconductivity ($\sigma_{Sxxx}$), Anomalous Hall Effect ($\sigma_{Axy}$), and the degree of valley polarization ($\langle	au_zangle$).
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength ($B$), layer number ($N$), and the strength of substrate-induced symmetry breaking ($\Delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on semiclassical transport theory, the length-gauge formalism for position operators, and approximations regarding the ensemble (canonical vs. grand canonical).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate bandstructures, phase diagrams showing the competition between different correlated phases (e.g., VPHM vs. SPHM), and quantitative comparisons of LMC vs. AHE responses across various doping levels and layer numbers.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (VP detection) to a detailed symmetry classification of transport responses (LMC vs. AHE). It then develops the theoretical framework using semiclassical transport and applies it to specific systems like TBG and RG, culminating in a comparative analysis of the two diagnostic tools.
why_interesting: The development of LMC as a robust, symmetry-protected diagnostic tool for intrinsic electronic order (like valley polarization) is highly relevant to understanding emergent quantum states in low-dimensional materials, which often exhibit complex many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
